chunk_summary: This section discusses the management of borderline anthropometric values, focusing on MUAC and WHZ as predictors of mortality and program outcomes in children under 5 years, with insights from CMAM initiatives and pooled analyses on wasting and stunting risks.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of MUAC and WHZ in predicting mortality and program outcomes in children under 5 years.
Analyze the impact of wasting and stunting as risk factors for child mortality based on pooled data.
Explore the implementation of CMAM strategies for improving child nutrition in outpatient care settings.
key_facts: MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) and WHZ (Weight-for-Height Z-score) are critical metrics for assessing malnutrition and predicting mortality in children under 5 years.
Wasting and stunting significantly increase mortality risk, as highlighted in pooled analyses (PLOS ONE, 2013).
Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) programs, supported by NITI Aayog and UNICEF, aim to transform child nutrition in India (2023 report).
MUAC thresholds for severe acute malnutrition are typically <115 mm, while borderline values require close monitoring.
Discordant metrics (e.g., normal MUAC but low WHZ) necessitate tailored interventions to address specific nutritional risks.
reference_quote: MUAC and WHZ are reliable predictors of mortality and program outcomes in children under 5 years, emphasizing the need for tailored interventions in outpatient care settings. (PLOS ONE, 2019)
